narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile, like your solid undergraduate GPA right around the typical 3.21 average and your upper-middle family income background. However, the admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on students' academic records, test scores, and backgrounds, estimated a 72.5% chance that you would fail the bar exam—high enough to lead to the rejection of your application. This prediction was driven primarily by your lower decile rankings in school grades, specifically your Year 3 performance being in the bottom decile compared to the average around the middle of the pack, which strongly pushed the model toward expecting failure. Your Year 1 grades, also in the lowest decile well below the typical mid-range, added to this concern as the next biggest factor influencing the decision. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA actually worked in your favor, pulling the prediction slightly toward success since it matched what successful students usually have. Your LSAT score of 31, though below the average of about 37, provided a modest positive nudge toward passing, while planning to attend full-time—common for 93% of students—helped a little too, as did your family income. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your early and later school deciles stand out as notably weaker, overshadowing those other solid areas. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black woman breaking barriers in law, but a data-driven flag on those key academic signals. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to the average, strengthening your high school-like decile performance through additional coursework or explanations in a reapplications, and gathering letters highlighting your resilience and growth. Many students in similar situations improve these metrics and succeed on reapplication—your story of perseverance can shine through with targeted steps. We're rooting for you to turn this into a comeback.
